analysis.problem: Cultural and media content is increasingly promoting divisive narratives and antisemitism, undermining social cohesion and trust in institutions.
analysis.root_cause: The dominance of media platforms and cultural institutions in shaping public discourse allows harmful narratives to spread, often without adequate scrutiny or accountability.
leverage_points: Media and cultural institutions that amplify divisive content
Lack of transparency and accountability in content moderation and curation

proposed_actions.action: Implement independent audits of major media platforms to assess the spread of divisive and antisemitic content
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Reduce the percentage of content flagged as antisemitic by 30% within one year

proposed_actions.action: Promote media literacy programs in schools and public spaces to help audiences critically engage with cultural and media content
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Increase public awareness of media manipulation tactics by 40% within two years

evidence: 'An orgy of antisemitism is overtaking the west': Son of Saul’s László Nemes on Hollywood hypocrisy
'It’s not healthy for me to be doing so much': from Adolescence to His Dark Materials, how Jack Thorne took over TV
analysis.generalization: This problem connects to the broader domain of information ecosystems, where the spread of harmful narratives can erode trust, polarize societies, and threaten democratic processes.
analysis.summary: Cultural and media content is spreading divisive and antisemitic narratives, requiring stronger accountability and media literacy to mitigate its impact.

raw_items.summary: Peter Gelb, the general manager of the Metropolitan Opera, said he is not going anywhere as he struggles with financial crises and opera’s place in a changing arts landscape.

raw_items.title: ‘An orgy of antisemitism is overtaking the west’: Son of Saul’s László Nemes on Hollywood hypocrisy
raw_items.summary:
His extraordinary Auschwitz film won every award going. Now the Hungarian director is back with new drama Orphan, as well as a Jean Moulin biopic at Cannes. He talks about resurgent global prejudice – and refusing to be lectured by the film industry ‘overclass’

raw_items.title: ‘It’s not healthy for me to be doing so much’: from Adolescence to His Dark Materials, how Jack Thorne took over TV
raw_items.summary:
He has made smash hit shows for Netflix and has adapted Harry Potter for the stage. Yet the in-demand writer’s latest show Falling is new territory for him – a love story

I hear Jack Thorne before I see him. We’re meeting in a quiet cafe in Hampstead, north London, to discuss his very first love story for TV, Falling. I catch snatches of him chatting with various waiters – “Thank you, good sir” and “Lovely, lovely” – before he appears in front of me, all smiles but a little ne

raw_items.title: Bulgaria wins 70th Eurovision contest with Dara and Bangaranga
raw_items.summary:
The UK finished last as the contest, held in Vienna, saw five countries boycott it over the participation of Israel

Bulgaria has won the 2026 Eurovision song contest after singer Dara swept to victory with the song Bangaranga.

The 27-year-old singer’s triumph is a first victory in the 70-year history of the song contest for Bulgaria, which only joined Eurovision in 2005 and sat out the last three editions.

raw_items.title: What should win this year’s International Booker?
raw_items.summary:
Political oppression in Tehran, a witch’s tale from France, a film-maker in Nazi Germany … we weigh up the contenders for the International Booker prize 2026

This year’s International Booker prize shortlisted titles are a diverse bunch, both geographically – from Brazil to Taiwan – and in style, from mainstream blockbuster to experimental jeu d’esprit.
